Metadata.Sector: Civil procedure – Third Party Proceedings; Companies – Substantial Shareholders; Companies – Directors – Duties; Tort – Conspiracy

Party_Details.Name: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd
Party_Details.Facts: Two related actions were brought in respect of the affairs of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd (AT). Suit No 477 of 2012 is a derivative action brought on behalf of AT by shareholder Carolyn Fong against former managing director Linda Kao Chai-Chau for alleged breaches of fiduciary duty in relation to certain transactions, mostly occurring while Peter Fong was alive and Chairman. Leave to commence the derivative action was granted under section 216A of the Companies Act after a finding that Peter Fong was the controlling mind and will of AT at the material times. After Suit 477 of 2012 was commenced, Ernst & Young was appointed as Receivers and Managers of AT on 17 January 2012 pursuant to an application by Carolyn and a subsequent consent order. The Receivers and Managers later formed the view that there was evidence of other diversions by Linda and others to the detriment of AT, largely concerning transactions or events after Peter Fong’s death, and commenced Suit No 1015 of 2012 against Linda and 15 other individuals, alleging conspiracy to divert business away from AT and breach of fiduciary duty. Different law firms represented AT in the two suits. In both suits, Linda’s defence was that the impugned transactions were carried out pursuant to the oral or standing instructions or understanding of Peter Fong, who was then majority shareholder, Chairman and alleged controlling mind and will of AT. In the derivative action, Linda applied for leave to serve a third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong for an indemnity, later seeking to amend to add a claim for contribution. In the RM action, Linda commenced third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong seeking an indemnity and later sought to amend to add a claim for contribution. The Assistant Registrar set aside the third party notice in the RM action and refused leave to commence third party proceedings in the derivative action. Linda appealed these decisions and also applied to amend the third party notices. The Estate of Peter Fong did not participate in either suit and did not object to the third party notices. The factual matrix includes allegations that Linda diverted business opportunities and funds away from AT, that some transactions occurred before and some after Peter Fong’s death, that Peter Fong had been appointed Chairman and was a substantial shareholder, that he died on 25 April 2008, and that shareholdings in AT had changed over time, including a transfer of a majority shareholding to the Fong Foundation charitable trust around 2006.
Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in the context of the two actions brought on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, the defendant managing director may pursue third party claims for indemnity or contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong in respect of the same damage alleged by AT, and whether such third party proceedings are precluded by illegality or redundancy and should be allowed to proceed.
Party_Details.Rule: Order 16 rule 1 of the Rules of Court governs the issuing of third party notices where a defendant claims contribution or indemnity, related relief, or determination of a question involving a non-party. Order 16 rules 4 and 6 empower the court to give directions and to terminate third party proceedings. Order 18 rule 19 permits striking out of pleadings that disclose no reasonable cause of action, are scandalous, frivolous or vexatious, may prejudice, embarrass or delay the fair trial, or are an abuse of process. Section 15(1) of the Civil Law Act provides that any person liable in respect of any damage suffered by another may recover contribution from any other person liable in respect of the same damage, whether jointly or otherwise. Section 19(2) of the Civil Law Act defines liability in respect of damage broadly to include liability in tort, contract, breach of trust or otherwise. Section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act gives the court discretion in contribution proceedings to exempt a person from liability to contribute or to direct that contribution amounts to a complete indemnity. Section 157 of the Companies Act imposes directors’ duties and provides that breach is an offence punishable by fine or imprisonment. Section 216A of the Companies Act provides for statutory derivative actions by shareholders with leave of court. The common law maxim ex turpi causa non oritur actio reflects a public policy that a person cannot found a claim on his own illegal act, and older authority such as Burrows v Rhodes and Jameson states that a person who knowingly commits an unlawful act cannot maintain an action for contribution or indemnity in respect of resulting liability. Case law including K and Another v P and Another and Nganthavee Teriya v Ang Yee Lim Lawrence interprets statutory contribution provisions as overriding common law restrictions based on illegality, while preserving judicial discretion on the quantum of contribution. Authorities such as Yemen Salt Mining Corp v Rhodes-Vaughan Steel Ltd, Westcoast Transmission Co v Interprovincial Steel & Pipe Corp, Nganthavee Teriya v Ang Yee Lim Lawrence, and Yong Kheng Leong v Panweld Trading Pte Ltd address when third party proceedings are redundant because the third party issues are fully subsumed within the main defence. Principles on directors’ fiduciary duties, the concept of a company’s controlling mind or alter ego, and the circumstances in which a director may avoid liability by full disclosure and shareholder assent are drawn from cases including Lim Leong Huat v Chip Hup Hup Kee Construction Pte Ltd, Belmont Finance Corporation Ltd v Williams Furniture Ltd, In re W & M Roith Ltd, and related authorities.
Party_Details.Application: The court examined whether Linda’s proposed third party claims against the Estate of Peter Fong for indemnity or contribution in respect of AT’s alleged losses had a legal and factual basis distinct from, and capable of surviving, the outcome of her defence in the main actions. On illegality, the court considered the plaintiffs’ reliance on the ex turpi causa principle and Burrows v Rhodes and Jameson, and contrasted this with the statutory scheme in sections 15, 16 and 19 of the Civil Law Act, as interpreted in K v P and Nganthavee, to assess whether statutory contribution claims are barred where the underlying conduct may involve torts or crimes. The court analysed that the statutory wording allows contribution wherever another person is liable for the same damage, regardless of whether the liability arises from tort, breach of contract, breach of trust or otherwise, and that any policy concerns about illegality can be addressed at the stage of apportioning or exempting contribution under section 16(2). On redundancy, the court compared the pleaded defences and the third party notices, and considered authorities such as Yemen, Westcoast, Nganthavee and Panweld, which indicate that third party proceedings may be unnecessary where success on the defence would eliminate any basis for contribution, and failure on the defence would likewise defeat the third party claim. The court evaluated whether there were realistic scenarios in which Linda might be found liable to AT for breach of her independent fiduciary and directors’ duties, yet the Estate of Peter Fong could also be found liable in respect of the same damage, for example if Peter Fong, as Chairman and director, had instructed or authorised the impugned transactions without being treated as the company’s alter ego, or if his conduct constituted a separate breach of duty to AT. The court considered the shareholding structure over time, the extent of Peter Fong’s control, the timing of the alleged diversions relative to his death and the transfer of shares to the Fong Foundation, and the differing positions taken by AT (Carolyn) and AT (RM) on whether Peter Fong was the controlling mind of AT. It also took into account principles that a director’s duties are owed to the company independently of instructions from a majority shareholder or chairman, and that a company may be treated as victim rather than co-conspirator where its directors act in fraud of it. The court assessed whether, on the assumed facts, there could be concurrent liability of Linda and the Estate of Peter Fong for the same loss to AT, justifying a contribution claim, and whether allowing third party proceedings would cause undue delay, expense or embarrassment to AT. The court further noted that any eventual adjustment of contribution, including possible exemption of a party or reduction to zero, would be a matter for the trial judge after full ventilation of the evidence and issues.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The court allowed the defendant’s appeals against the Assistant Registrar’s decisions, permitted the commencement and continuation of third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong in both suits, and granted the applications to amend the third party notices to include claims for contribution in addition to indemnity.
